perturbation.original_text: Any late payments under this Agreement will be assessed a service fee of one and one-half percent (1.5%) per month, to the extent allowed by law.
perturbation.changed_text: Any late payments under this Agreement will be assessed a service fee of twenty percent (20%) per month.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text imposes a late payment fee of 20% per month, which exceeds the maximum interest rate allowed by California law for consumer contracts and potentially usurious under California law.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Constitution Article XV Section 1 limits interest rates for loans and forbearance to 10% per year, unless there is an exemption. The 20% monthly fee translates to 240% per year, which is usurious and violates the California Constitution.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text * California Constitution - CONS ARTICLE XV USURY [SECTION 1- SECTION 1.] ( Article 15 added June 8, 1976, by Prop. 14, clause 35. Res.Ch. 5, 1976. ) SECTION 1. The rate of interest upon the loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, or on accounts after demand, shall be 7 percent per annum but it shall be competent for the parties to any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action to contract in writing for a rate of interest: (1) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action, if the money, goods, or things in action are for use primarily for personal, family, or household purposes, at a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum; provided, however, that any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action the proceeds of which are used primarily for the purchase, construction or improvement of real property shall not be deemed to be a use primarily for personal, family or household purposes; or (2) For any loan or forbearance of any money, goods, or things in action for any use other than specified in paragraph (1), at a rate not exceeding the higher of (a) 10 percent per annum or (b) 5 percent per annum plus the rate prevailing on the 25th day of the month preceding the earlier of (i) the date of execution of the contract to make the loan or forbearance, or (ii) the date of making the loan or forbearance established by the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco on advances to member banks under Sections 13 and 13a of the Federal Reserve Act as now in effect or hereafter from time to time amended (or if there is no such single determinable rate of advances, the closest counterpart of such rate as shall be designated by the Superintendent of Banks of the State of California unless some other person or agency is delegated such authority by the Legislature). No person, association, copartnership or corporation shall by charging any fee, bonus, commission, discount or other compensation receive from a borrower more than the interest authorized by this section upo
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts California Constitution Article XV Section 1, which limits interest rates to 10% per annum for loans primarily for personal, family, or household purposes. The changed text imposes a 20% monthly fee, which equates to 240% per year. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states: "...at a rate not exceeding 10 percent per annum; provided, however, that any loan or forbearance of any money, goods or things in action the proceeds of which are used primarily for the purchase, construction or improvement of real property shall not be deemed to be a use primarily for personal, family or household purposes". The modification introduces a rate significantly exceeding the legal limit, leading to a clear legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California without reference to conflict of law principles thereof. Any claim arising out of or related to this Agreement must be brought exclusively in the state or federal courts located in San Diego County, California, and each party hereby consent to the jurisdiction thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware without reference to conflict of law principles thereof. Any claim arising out of or related to this Agreement must be brought exclusively in the courts located in Delaware County, Delaware, and each party hereby consent to the jurisdiction thereof.
perturbation.explanation: The original text stipulates California law and jurisdiction. The modified text changes this to Delaware, despite both parties being located and operating primarily in California. This may be unenforceable under California law if it deprives a California resident of the protections of California law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 410.42
perturbation.law_citation: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 410.42
perturbation.law_explanation: CCP 410.42 states that a contract provision that specifies exclusive jurisdiction in another state is unenforceable if the individual bringing the action resides or resided in California when the contract was entered into, the other state is not California, and the contract arises from the provision of services to the individual primarily in California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 410.42 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. JURISDICTION AND SERVICE OF PROCESS [410.10 - 418.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1969, Ch. 1610. ) CHAPTER 1. Jurisdiction and Forum [410.10 - 410.70] ( Heading of Chapter 1 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 3. ) ARTICLE 2. Forum [410.30 - 410.42] ( Heading of Article 2 amended by Stats. 1992, Ch. 615, Sec. 4. ) 410.42. (a) The following provisions of a contract between the contractor and a subcontractor with principal offices in this state, for the construction of a public or private work of improvement in this state, shall be void and unenforceable: (1) A provision which purports to require any dispute between the parties to be litigated, arbitrated, or otherwise determined outside this state. (2) A provision which purports to preclude a party from commencing such a proceeding or obtaining a judgment or other resolution in this state or the courts of this state. (b) For purposes of this section, “construction” means any work or services performed on, or materials provided for, a work of improvement, as defined in Section 8050 of the Civil Code, and for which a lien may be claimed pursuant to Section 8400 of the Civil Code (whether or not a lien is in fact cl
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the agreement's governing law and jurisdiction from California to Delaware. The Perturbation and Law explanations state that California Code of Civil Procedure (CCP) 410.42 addresses the enforceability of such provisions. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which is an excerpt from CCP 410.42, indicates that a contract provision requiring disputes to be resolved outside of California is void and unenforceable under specific conditions: the contractor and subcontractor have principal offices in California, and the construction is for a public or private work of improvement in California. The changed text designating Delaware as the jurisdiction contradicts CCP 410.42 if the conditions outlined in the law are met, making it unenforceable. The snippet explicitly states that such a provision 'shall be void and unenforceable.'

perturbation.original_text: Tickets agrees to accept MP3.com's measurement of Impressions (the "Count") according to MP3.com's logs and other tracking devices and/or software MP3.com may use, provided however, that Tickets shall have the right to audit MP3.com's records in this regard.
perturbation.changed_text: Tickets agrees to accept MP3.com's measurement of Impressions (the "Count") according to MP3.com's logs and other tracking devices and/or software MP3.com may use, and Tickets waives any right to audit MP3.com's records in this regard.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text removes Tickets' right to audit MP3.com's records regarding impressions. While parties can generally waive contractual rights, doing so without clear consideration or understanding could be challenged under California law, especially if there's a significant power imbalance or potential for fraud.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code Section 1668 states that contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt anyone from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. While seemingly unrelated, by removing the audit provision, the contract makes it much easier for MP3.com to commit fraud and potentially violate the law without being detected, which goes against public policy.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1668 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 4. UNLAWFUL CONTRACTS [1667 - 1670.12] ( Title 4 enacted 1872. ) 1668. All contracts which have for their object, directly or indirectly, to exempt any one from responsibility for his own fraud, or willful injury to the person or property of another, or violation of law, whether willful or negligent, are against the policy of the law. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the right of Tickets to audit MP3.com's records. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both suggest this removal creates a situation where MP3.com could more easily commit fraud without detection. Scraped Law Snippet 1, California Civil Code Section 1668, states that contracts that indirectly exempt anyone from responsibility for their own fraud are against public policy. By removing the audit provision, the contract indirectly makes it easier for MP3.com to commit fraud, thus contradicting Section 1668. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition.
